--- SOURCE 1: Single Atom Catalysts: A Review of Characterization Methods - 2021 ---
URL: https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020

SUMMARY:
This review addresses this challenge – identifying catalytically relevant features of physicochemical properties of single atoms (charge state, electronic structure, atomic configuration, bonding interactions with a support) and surveying advanced tools/methods for characterizing them. Several recent reports summarized specific methods, such as extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) spectroscopy for studying coordination environments in Pt SACs on diverse supports.10 The literature also describes useful methods for probing electronic structures associated with metal-support interactions in SACs.11 In this review, we discuss in depth the characterization techniques for single atom catalysts, focused on addressing key chemical questions: What structural, dynamic and electronic attributes of SACs affect their activity, selectivity, stability and, in general, catalytic reaction mechanisms? 15 J. 23 J. 29 J. 36 J. 41a J. 44a J.

Graphical Abstract
------------------

**Single atom catalysts** harbor a potential to exceed nanoparticle catalysts in terms of activity, stability and selectivity in a growing number of chemical reactions. This review addresses the challenges in identifying the catalytically relevant features of physiochemical properties of single atoms and surveys advanced tools/methods for characterizing them.

[![Image 6: Description unavailable](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/8eef67c4-d9fe-462e-84a1-0f35e4132bac/cmtd202100020-toc-0001-m.png)](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/6b1b6a3a-0712-4fb6-bff8-5bae49590a06/cmtd202100020-toc-0001-m.jpg)

Abstract
--------

Single atom catalysts (SACs) harbor a potential to exceed nanoparticle catalysts in terms of activity, stability and selectivity in a growing number of chemical reactions. Although their investigation is attracting significant attention, important fundamental questions focusing on key physicochemical properties of SACs (e. g., structure – property relationships, structural dynamics, reaction-driven restructuring) remain unanswered. A main challenge for research in the field is how to reliably characterize the environments of single atoms in the presence of complicating factors such as low weight loadings, strong metal-support interactions, and atomic and multiscale heterogeneity of bonding in the single atom sites. This review addresses this challenge – identifying catalytically relevant features of physicochemical properties of single atoms (charge state, electronic structure, atomic configuration, bonding interactions with a support) and surveying advanced tools/methods for characterizing them. The review places a strong emphasis on multimodal methods exploiting X-ray absorption, emission and photoelectron spectroscopies, and provides several examples from the authors’ research that demonstrate their use as powerful tools for SAC characterization.

1 Introduction
--------------

Single atom heterogeneous catalysts (SACs) have attracted much attention in the last decade, primarily because they have shown high activity, selectivity, and stability in a growing number of chemical reactions.[1](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-bib-0001) After the concept of a SAC was first introduced by T. Zhang and co-workers,[2](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-bib-0002) their investigation experienced rapid growth in fields spanning diverse forms of heterogeneous catalysis including notable examples of thermolytic, photo, and electro catalysis. The embodiments of bonding and composition in SAC materials are both complex and diverse, having evolved well beyond the embedding of isolated atoms at the interface of an active host matrix (mostly those of metal oxides) in the early stages of their investigation to metals (giving rise to single atom alloys, or SAAs[1](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-bib-0001)), metal organic frameworks, carbon nitride and other organic framework-based materials, among many others.[3](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-bib-0003) Current research in the field continues to expand the range of SAC structures with useful activities and selectivity in catalytic transformations as shown in Figure [1](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-fig-0001). The diversity of these exemplary SACs ranges from single metal atoms anchored on the surface of a mesoporous reducible oxide[4](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-bib-0007) (Figure [1](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-fig-0001)a) to isolated surface metal atoms as part of an alloy[5](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-bib-0008) (Figure [1](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-fig-0001)b) to metal atoms supported on single layer materials[6](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-bib-0009) (Figure [1](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-fig-0001)c). While “single atom catalysts” is the ubiquitous term used to describe these systems, the phrase “atomically dispersed catalysts” may better convey that the individual metal atoms are located at the surface of another material. There are several earlier reviews that have explored recent developments in studies of SAC activity, such as capturing reaction intermediates and monitoring the dynamics of both the geometric structure and electronic environment of atomically dispersed sites,[7](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-bib-0010) and optimizing their reactivity by tailoring the local environment of active sites and speciations of the metal ions they embed.[8](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-bib-0011) Similar advances in the use of SACs for electrocatalysis have also been reviewed.[9](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-bib-0012) In general terms, progress in the SAC field has gone hand in hand with an increasing recognition of the various forms of complexity that are exhibited by these materials – initially unexpected due to the assumed “homogeneity” of their atomically dispersed sites. As such, unveiling the structure-property relationships in SACs is hindered by both the inherent heterogeneity of the metal sites present in SACs and the paucity of experimental methods capable of probing the attributes of their bonding structures with adsorbates and support in presence of such heterogeneity.

Several recent reports summarized specific methods, such as extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) spectroscopy for studying coordination environments in Pt SACs on diverse supports.[10](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-bib-0013) The literature also describes useful methods for probing electronic structures associated with metal-support interactions in SACs.[11](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-bib-0014) In this review, we discuss in depth the characterization techniques for single atom catalysts, focused on addressing key chemical questions: What structural, dynamic and electronic attributes of SACs affect their activity, selectivity, stability and, in general, catalytic reaction mechanisms? To address these questions, we outline methods, and their combinations, that are effective for characterizing speciations of SACs, measure oxidation states of metal and support atoms, characterize their electronic structure and properties, and provide quantitative information about the local bonding/coordination environments. We also highlight the implications of coexisting multiple binding sites for SACs that complicate interpretation of ensemble averaging techniques, such as EXAFS spectroscopy. The sections below develop these points in turn and demonstrate how multimodal methods are usefully integrated to characterize them.

2 Properties and Approaches to their Characterization
-----------------------------------------------------

### 2.1 Oxidation States of Metal and Support Atoms

Characterization of oxidation states of SACs and support atoms comprising their nearest environment is essential to probe the nature of the metal-support interactions that are now understood to highly impact the stability and activity of supported SACs. The correlation between metal loadings, surface defects, and the oxidation states of single atoms on a support has been widely investigated.[12](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-bib-0015) Single metal atoms may show different oxidation states at different coordination environments, as shown in Figure [2](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-fig-0002)a. Ye et al.,[7](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-bib-0010) prepared two types of ceria nanorod supports with two different concentrations of Ce 3+ surface defect sites (noted as x%Pt 1/D−CeO 2 with relatively higher Ce 3+ components and x%Pt 1/C−CeO 2 with relatively lower Ce 3+ components). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results shown in Figure [2](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-fig-0002)b indicated that Pt atoms anchored on defect sites are mainly in a Pt 2+ valence state, while those on terrace sites are mainly in a Pt 4+ state. Additionally, Kunwar et al.[13](https://chemistry-europe.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/cmtd.202100020#cmtd202100020-bib-0019) found that Pt species in ceria supported Pt single atom catalysts with high Pt loading (3 wt.%) were primarily in the Pt 2+ oxidation state with a fraction of Pt 4+also being observed. When the Pt loading was increased to 4 wt.%, an obvious metallic Pt peak coexisting with the ionic Pt 2+ was observed. ### Abstract

Single atom catalysts (SACs) are a hot research area recently. Over most of the SACs, the singly dispersed atoms are the active sites, which contribute to the catalytic activities significantly compared with a catalyst with continuously packed active sites. It is essential to determine whether SACs have been successfully synthesized. Several techniques have been applied for the characterization of the dispersion states of the active sites over SACs, such as Energy Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), Electron Energy Loss Spectroscopy (EELS), *etc.* In this review, the techniques for the identification of the singly dispersed sites over SACs are introduced, the advantages and limitations of each technique are pointed out, and the future research directions have been discussed. It is hoped that this review will be helpful for a more comprehensive understanding of the characterization and detection methods involved in SACs, and stimulate and promote the further development of this emerging research field.

# Direct quantitative assessment of single-atom metal sites supported on powder catalysts

[*Communications Materials*](/commsmat)
**volume 5**, Article number: 206 (2024)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

2083 Accesses

1 Citations

1 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

Rational design of effective catalyst demands a profound understanding of active-site structures. Single-atom supported powder catalysts, depicting unique features like enhanced metal dispersion, hold promise in different applications. Here, we present an approach to directly quantify the detailed structural nature of metal sites in single-atom, high surface area, powder catalysts. By combining advanced high-resolution scanning-transmission electron microscopy, deep learning and density functional theory calculations, we determine, with statistical significance, the exact location and coordination environment of Pd single-atoms supported on MgO nanoplates. Our findings reveal a preferential interaction of Pd single-atoms with cationic vacancies (V-centers), followed by occupation of anionic defects on the {001} MgO surface. The former interaction results in stabilization of PdO species within V-centers, while partially embedded Pd states are found in F-defects. This methodology opens a route to the ultimate structural analysis of metal-support interaction effects, key in the design of advanced nanocatalysts for sustainable and energy-efficient processes.

## Introduction

The use of single atoms catalysts (SACs) has attracted a large attention because of their excellent properties, when compared to their nanoparticles counterpart, in terms of reactivity in many different reactions, particularly in the field of Environmental Catalysis and Clean Energy[1](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR1 "Ding, S. P., Hülsey, M. J., Pérez-Ramírez, J. & Yang, N. Transforming energy with single-atom catalysts. Joule 3, 2897–2929 (2019)."),[2](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR2 "Beniya, A. et al. CO oxidation activity of non-reducible oxide-supported mass-selected few-atom Pt single-clusters. Nat. Commun. 11, 1888 (2020)."). These species are supported either on non-porous structures, typically oxides or carbon-based materials, or hosted into crystalline porous materials, such as zeolites or metal-organic frameworks (MOFs). Nonetheless, the performance of these species are highly influenced by the local atomic environment, *i.e*. the specific interaction with the support, and the structural characteristics of these metal entities[3](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR3 "Mitchell, S. & Pérez-Ramírez, J. Atomically precise control in the design of low-nuclearity supported metal catalysts. Nat. Rev. Mater. 6, 969–985 (2021)."). In particular, when a non-porous structured material is employed as support, the large heterogeneity of the surfaces exposed by the crystallites and the presence of surface defects provide a large variety of anchoring sites for the atomically-dispersed species, with differing electronic and geometric properties and, therefore, distinct catalytic performances.

The exact structural characteristics of these differing sites are mainly related to both metal-metal and metal-support interactions. Hence, tailoring these structural characteristics opens a route to modulate the reactivity of this type of catalysts. In this sense, great efforts have been paid not only in the development of synthetic methods to control as much as possible structural features but also in the development of characterization techniques enabling a discrimination of key aspects like atomicity, composition, geometry and local environment of the metal species[4](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR4 "Liu, L. C. & Corma, A. Confining isolated atoms and clusters in crystalline porous materials for catalysis. Nat. Rev. Mater. 6, 244–263 (2021)."),[5](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR5 "Büchele, S. et al. Elucidation of metal local environments in single-atom catalysts based on carbon nitrides. Small 18, 2202080 (2022).").

Regarding characterization, high resolution high angle annular dark Field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HR HAADF-STEM) imaging has consolidated as the only tool to directly visualize single metal atoms in high surface area, powder type, catalysts. The unique characterization capabilities of HAADF-STEM rely on the two following questions: (i) it allows imaging matter with sub-Angstrom spatial resolution, (ii) the signal collected to build the image is proportional to roughly the squared atomic number Z[2](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR2 "Beniya, A. et al. CO oxidation activity of non-reducible oxide-supported mass-selected few-atom Pt single-clusters. Nat. Commun. 11, 1888 (2020)."). Therefore, it provides images with chemical contrast[6](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR6 "Kalinin, S. V. et al. Machine learning in scanning transmission electron microscopy. Nat. Rev. Methods Prim. 2, 11 (2022)."). However, most studies reported in recent years exploiting HAADF-STEM images to detect single metal atoms or subnanometric metal clusters supported on non-porous or porous oxides, use this technique simply in a qualitative way[7](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR7 "Liu, L. C. & Corma, A. Metal catalysts for heterogeneous catalysis: from single atoms to nanoclusters and nanoparticles. Chem. Rev. 118, 4981–5079 (2018)."). In fact, only a few reports extract quantitative information about relevant structural features of SACs. Among these, we should mention the work of Chang et al., focused on the atomic-scale examination of the local coordination environments of isolated Pt atoms in various single-atom catalyst (SAC)[8](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR8 "Chang, T. Y. et al. Direct Imaging of Pt single atoms adsorbed on TiO2 (110) surfaces. Nano Lett. 14, 134–138 (2014)."). In this case, a preferential anchoring of Pt atoms at vacancy sites neighboring basal oxygen atoms in subsurface positions was reported. While this study provided valuable insights, SACs were manually identified on the images, a strategy that does not only limit the size of the observation set but also introduces some degree of subjectivity when selecting each observation. Moreover, the sample preparation method used in this study, which involved vacuum evaporation of Pt, diverges significantly from the more prevalent wet impregnation technique routinely used to prepare metal-SACs supported on oxides.

In another contribution, De Rita et al. delved into the local coordination environments of isolated Pt atoms supported on anatase-TiO2 using in-situ and ex-situ scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) as well as spectroscopic techniques. Their findings pointed out to a preferential Pt-Ti interaction, particularly evident under hydrogen environment[9](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR9 "DeRita, L. et al. Structural evolution of atomically dispersed Pt catalysts dictates reactivity. Nat. Mater. 18, 746–751 (2019).").

More recently, Büchel *et al*. explored the metal local environment of Pt atoms on carbon nitrides, employing a comprehensive battery of techniques including HR-HAADF microscopy, density functional theory (DFT) calculations, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR). However, they recognized limitations to provide information about the local metal environment with statistical significance. As a result, they claim for the implementation of automated methods to extract this crucial data[5](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR5 "Büchele, S. et al. Elucidation of metal local environments in single-atom catalysts based on carbon nitrides. Small 18, 2202080 (2022)."). Regarding this suggestion, state-of-the-art algorithms, based on artificial intelligence (AI) methods, are opening new opportunities in the automated analysis of digital images. If properly applied to aberration-corrected (AC)-STEM experiments, tools based on this approach could provide additional and relevant knowledge about the structural characteristics and local atomic environment of complex materials. Actually, most current trends clearly point out that AI techniques will become an indispensable tool for the ultimate analysis of STEM data, particularly in the case of large datasets[6](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR6 "Kalinin, S. V. et al. Machine learning in scanning transmission electron microscopy. Nat. Rev. Methods Prim. 2, 11 (2022)."). In materials with an intrinsic heterogeneity as large as that commonly present in powder-type supported catalysts, the analysis of large volume datasets becomes a must if significant conclusions about structure are sought.

Among AI techniques, those based on deep learning (DL) methods are getting an increasing relevance[10](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR10 "Chen, K. & Barnard, A. S. Advancing electron microscopy using deep learning. J. Phys. Mater. 7, 022001 (2024)."). DL proceeds by analyzing a huge amount of data. To process these data, AI algorithms are trained using models with features similar to those present in the input data to be analyzed. This training step is crucial when we are dealing with single atoms catalysts and, in general, key to get a good performance of supervised DL algorithms. Although many different DL methods are available, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are to date those showing the best performance and most widely used for processing digital images. In particular, CNNs methods have been very recently used to denoise, segment and identify features of interest in HAADF-STEM images[11](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR11 "Botifoll, M., Pinto-Huguet, I. & Arbiol, J. Machine learning in electron microscopy for advanced nanocharacterization: current developments, available tools and future outlook. Nanoscale Horiz. 7, 1427–1477 (2022).").

In the particular case of SACs, Mitchell et al. used CNNs to statistically characterize Pt and Fe SACs supported on carbon nitride[12](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR12 "Mitchell, S. et al. Automated image analysis for single-atom detection in catalytic materials by transmission electron microscopy. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 144, 8018–8029 (2022)."). More recently, Ni et al. also employed a CNN architecture to quantify the spatial arrangement of various adatom configurations supported on a carbon substrate, specifically focusing on determining the atomicity of these species with statistical significance[13](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR13 "Ni, H. Y. et al. Quantifying atomically dispersed catalysts using deep learning assisted microscopy. Nano Lett. 23, 7442–7448 (2023)."). However, these studies do not evaluate the local coordination environment of the metal; a quite important feature, intimately connected to the actual nature of metal-support interactions. To reveal this key aspect, it is necessary to determine in quantitative terms subtler details of the metal site structure, a quite challenging task in single-atom supported powder catalysts.

Our work faces this challenge, focusing specifically on Pd SACs anchored onto a high surface area MgO support; a family of catalysts with promising potential applications in various reactions within the field of Catalysis, as reported in recent contributions. Thus, Sarma et al. have investigated the catalytic activity of Pd SACs supported on MgO for CO oxidation, attributing the catalytic performance to the presence of oxidized Pd species[14](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR14 "Sarma, B. B. et al. Metal-specific reactivity in single-atom catalysts: CO oxidation on 4d and 5d transition metals atomically dispersed on MgO. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 142, 14890–14902 (2020)."). Another study, by Guo and co-workers[15](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR15 "Guo, Y. L. et al. High performance of single-atom catalyst Pd1/MgO for semi-hydrogenation of acetylene to ethylene in excess ethylene. ChemNanoMat 7, 526–529 (2021)."), demonstrated that Pd SACs supported on MgO exhibited high performance in the semi-hydrogenation of acetylene to ethylene. More recently, Chen et al. studied the catalytic activity of Pd SACs supported on MgO for ethylene hydrogenation. They identified two distinct Pd species: Pd cations located at the MgO steps and Pd atoms embedded in the first subsurface layer of MgO, attributing the best catalytic activity to the species found at the steps. This work underscores the key influence of the exact location of Pd atoms on catalytic performance, highlighting the need for a more in-depth analysis of structural features and metal-support interactions[16](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR16 "Chen, Y. Z. et al. Dynamic structural evolution of MgO-supported palladium catalysts: from metal to metal oxide nanoparticles to surface then subsurface atomically dispersed cations. Chem. Sci. 15, 6454–6464 (2024).").

As a major goal, our study aims at evaluating, with statistical significance and high accuracy, the exact location of the metal species in support crystallites with intricate texture. To this end, we explore the combined use of complementary CNN algorithms with DFT calculations for a reliable and fully quantitative assessment of the ultimate structural features of metal-support interactions in SACs prepared on powder type, high surface area, non-porous oxide supports. It should be also noticed that the results obtained by combining CNN algorithms with DFT calculations were complemented and matched with others coming from macroscopic techniques like XPS, to provide a more complete picture of the single atom-support interactions responsible of the final performance of these systems. The methodology here developed, amenable to many other supported metal systems, contributes to bridging the gap between theoretical predictions and experimental observations, thereby advancing the field of heterogeneous catalysis in terms of fundamental knowledge.

## Results and discussion

### Study of the crystallographic nature of the surface exposed by the support

Evaluating metal-support interactions in powdered catalysts requires knowledge about the crystallography of the surface exposed by the support crystallites, a task of considerable complexity. In this study a high surface area MgO support was chosen due to the possibility of eventually controlling the surface properties and, at the same time, facilitating a high dispersion of the metal atoms. Furthermore, the low atomic number of the constituting elements, *Z*Mg = 12 and *Z*O = 8, makes of MgO a good choice for observing and analyzing individual atoms at the atomic scale with HAADF-STEM.

The support was prepared transforming commercial MgO into Mg(OH)2 via a hydrothermal method, followed by a post-treatment in H2(5%)/He at 900 °C for 6 h to achieve the final plate-like morphology. The X-ray powder diffraction diagram (PXRD), Supplementary Fig. [1a, b](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#MOESM1), reveals the peaks of periclase, indicating a complete transformation of Mg(OH)2 into the corresponding oxide. Refinement of the experimental PXRD data reveals that the support consists of MgO crystals approximately 25 nm in size. BET measurements provided a specific surface area of 80 m2/g, Supplementary Fig. [2](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#MOESM1). A simple calculation considering the density of MgO indicates that an 80 m2/g surface area requires 21 nm crystallites, a value quite close to that determined by PXRD

Textural features, encompassing external morphology and crystallographic planes exposed at the surface were analyzed using HAADF-STEM electron tomography (ET) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HREM). Figure [1a](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#Fig1) displays the volume rendering of a representative reconstruction of a MgO nanocrystal. Note that the crystallites depict an overall hexagonal morphology and that they are built up by the assembly of a huge number of small, roughly 25 nm in size, nano-cubes, in good agreement with PXRD analysis. The result is a porous texture, as clearly observed in the orthoslices extracted from the reconstructed volume, Fig. [1b–d](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#Fig1).

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs43246-024-00652-8/MediaObjects/43246_2024_652_Fig1_HTML.png)

Volume rendering of the HAADF-STEM electron tomography reconstruction of MgO nanoplates **a**; slices cut through the reconstructed volume along the XY **b**, XZ **c** and YZ **d** planes; **e** Aberration-corrected HREM image of a MgO nanoplate; **f** Digital Diffraction Pattern obtained from the red-dashed square in (**c**).

Figure [1e](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#Fig1) displays a representative aberration-corrected high-resolution electron microscopy (AC-HREM) image of a MgO hexagonal-shaped aggregate. The digital diffraction pattern (DDP), extracted from the marked area, reveals distances and angles consistent with the {200} planes in the <001> zone axis of MgO. Note that the MgO crystallite is made up, in good agreement with ET, by small cubes limited by stepped {001} facets. Importantly, these textural features agree perfectly with the mechanism proposed by Thomele et al.[17](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR17 "Thomele, D. et al. Cubes to cubes: organization of MgO particles into one-dimensional and two-dimensional nanostructures. Cryst. Growth Des. 21, 4674–4682 (2021)."). These authors reported that after annealing at 900 °C and exposure to O2, MgO nanostructures derived from the transformation of Mg(OH)2, fully convert into MgO(100) nanocubes due to the removal of the hydroxyl groups responsible for stabilizing the MgO (111) surfaces. Consequently, based on the results obtained from our analysis and considering the mechanism proposed, the crystallographic surface exposed by the MgO nanoplates is predominantly {001}.

### Quantitative assessment of SACs-Support interactions

The Pd SACs were supported on the MgO {001} nanoplates by using wet chemical methods (see methods section) and further treated in O2/He at 500 °C. PXRD (Supplementary Fig. [1 b](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#MOESM1)) and BET technique (Supplementary Fig. [3](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#MOESM1)) indicate that there is no significant modification of the support structure or texture after preparing the catalyst.

Figure [2](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#Fig2) gathers representative HR-HAADF STEM images acquired on the Pd catalyst supported on MgO {001} nanoplates, recorded along four major MgO crystallographic orientations: <100 > , Fig. [2a](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#Fig2), <110 > , Fig. [2b](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#Fig2), <111 > , Fig. [2c](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#Fig2) and <211 > , Fig. [2d](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#Fig2). Notably, all the images reveal the presence of bright spots with different intensity levels, as shown in the profiles extracted from images along the different orientations, Supplementary Fig. [4](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#MOESM1). Based on the contrast mechanism inherent to HAADF imaging, the more intense spots can be attributed to sites where individual Pd atoms locate (*Z*Pd = 46), while the less bright ones should correspond to Pd-free, either pure Mg columns (*Z*Mg = 12) or mixed Mg-O columns. Finally, the darker regions would locate on pure oxygen anionic columns (*Z*O = 8). It is important to highlight that the very light O columns exhibit weak signals and cannot be effectively captured in HAADF mode.

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs43246-024-00652-8/MediaObjects/43246_2024_652_Fig2_HTML.png)

 < 100> **a**; <110> **b**; <111> **c** and <211> **d** zone axis. Insets show the corresponding DDPs. Specific regions extracted from the HAADF images for <100> **e**, <110> **f**, <111> **g** and <211> **h** orientations are also shown. Scale bar is 2 nm.

To explore the influence of the starting metal precursor on the final structure, different catalysts were prepared. The differences observed in the number of individual species per surface unit between the different images is linked to this synthesis parameter. Specifically, images obtained along <100> (Fig. [2a](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#Fig2)), <110> (Fig. [2b](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#Fig2)), and <111> (Fig. [2c](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#Fig2)) come from a catalyst prepared from a Pd(II) complex, whereas the image recorded along <211> (Fig. [2d](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#Fig2)) was recorded on a sample in which Pd(NO3)2 was used as precursor. As commented in more detail below, no other influence of the metal precursor on the catalyst structure was observed.

For a more detailed examination of these species, specific regions were selected from each image in the complete set of HR-HAADF STEM results, as illustrated in Fig. [2e–h](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#Fig2). Regardless of the precursor, the contrasts due to Pd SACs consistently locate, in all the images, on atomic columns containing Mg2+ cations rather than just O. Importantly, the applied imaging conditions did not lead to noticeable beam-induced alterations in the position of the atomic contrasts, as is shown in Supplementary Fig. [5](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#MOESM1). In fact, this result agrees with previous HR-HAADF studies performed on highly dispersed transition metals supported either on MgO (111) nanoplates[18](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR18 "Fang, H. H. et al. Dispersed surface Ru ensembles on MgO(111) for catalytic ammonia decomposition. Nat. Commun. 14, 647 (2023).") or MgO nanocrystals[14](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR14 "Sarma, B. B. et al. Metal-specific reactivity in single-atom catalysts: CO oxidation on 4d and 5d transition metals atomically dispersed on MgO. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 142, 14890–14902 (2020)."),[19](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR19 "Aydin, C., Kulkarni, A., Chi, M. F., Browning, N. D. & Gates, B. C. Atomically resolved site-isolated catalyst on MgO: mononuclear osmium dicarbonyls formed from Os3(CO)12. J. Phys. Chem. Lett. 3, 1865–1871 (2012).").

The expectation is that the location and arrangement of the metallic species is mainly influenced by their specific interactions with the support. These will depend on factors like the electronic state of the metallic atoms, the characteristics of the crystallographic surface and the presence of defects in the support, among others[3](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR3 "Mitchell, S. & Pérez-Ramírez, J. Atomically precise control in the design of low-nuclearity supported metal catalysts. Nat. Rev. Mater. 6, 969–985 (2021).").

Turning our attention specifically onto the location of Pd species on the MgO nanoplates, just those images in both <110> and <211> zone axis (Fig. [2f](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#Fig2) and h), in which the atomic columns comprise only either Mg or O atoms, as illustrated in Supplementary Fig. [6c, d](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#MOESM1), offer the potential to distinguish the nature of the interaction at play, see Supporting information for further explanation.

In fact, in the experimental images recorded along both these orientations, Pd species locate on the atomic columns whose contrasts can be attributed to pure cationic columns. Importantly, simulated HR-HAADF images, Supplementary Fig. [7](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#MOESM1), confirm that Pd atoms could be visualized in both Mg and O positions even in the case of 36 nm thick crystals. Indeed, Supplementary Fig. [7d](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#MOESM1) plots the variation of the intensity in different locations of the simulated images as a function of MgO crystal thickness, indicating that the detection of a Pd atom on a Mg column could be extended much further, since there is still a considerable difference between the intensities of the contrasts of these two types of columns in the case of the thickest, 36 nm, crystal.

On the other hand, it is important to mention that the HREM image in Fig. [1](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#Fig1) clearly reveals steps on the {001} surfaces of the nanocubes. However, the HAADF-STEM images were recorded in planar view, in which Pd atoms are predominantly located in the central areas of the crystals. The question arises if the steps can be effectively and reliably detected under these particular imaging conditions. Therefore, to further investigate the presence of steps on the {001} surface, HR-HAADF STEM image simulations in the <211> orientation were conducted, as illustrated in Supplementary Fig. [8](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#MOESM1). Specifically, the simulated images of two different structural models were obtained and analyzed. In one of these models, a Pd atom was placed in a Mg vacancy on a completely flat surface (Supplementary Fig. [8a](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#MOESM1)). In a second one, a step was added just on top of the Pd atom (Supplementary Fig. [8b](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#MOESM1)). In addition, to ensure that the simulations closely resembled the experimental data, we considered the characteristics of the HAADF detector and incorporated the noise present in the experimental images into the simulated ones.

Notably, in the noise-free HAADF-STEM image simulations, the images exhibit quite similar contrasts, making it difficult to determine whether the Pd atoms are located or not at steps. As clearly shown in this figure, the comparison of the intensity profiles extracted from both simulated images indicates only subtle intensity changes in the intensity of the Mg columns on the model sites where the step is present. In fact, in the presence of noise, these differences are completely wiped out, rendering the distinction between the two situations not viable, as indicated by the corresponding intensity profiles. Therefore, there will very likely be steps in the sample area imaged in Fig. [2](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#Fig2) but, according to these simulations, it is not possible neither to detect where these locate nor if Pd is associated to this type of defect.

To improve the analysis of these observations and accurately quantify the nature of metal-support interaction, with statistical robustness, a smart segmentation procedure was developed to analyze the HR-HAADF images in <211> orientation. This segmentation approach involved partitioning the images into its distinct constituents: the background and the objects of interest, *i,e* the atomic columns containing Pd single atoms and those containing only Mg. As previously mentioned, DL tools, particularly those used in the domain of computer vision, have proven highly beneficial for this image processing task. In particular, semantic segmentation methodologies have been harnessed to generate segmentation maps which effectively translate pixel values into class labels.

Since convolutional neural networks employing the U-net architecture have demonstrated remarkable performance in this task[20](#ref-CR20 "Yang, S. H. et al. Deep learning morphological distribution analysis of metal alloy catalysts in proton exchange membrane fuel cells. Mater. Today Energy 36, 101348 (2023)."),[21](#ref-CR21 "Faraz, K., Grenier, T., Ducottet, C. & Epicier, T. Deep learning detection of nanoparticles and multiple object tracking of their dynamic evolution during in situ ETEM studies. Sci. Rep. 12, 2484 (2022)."),[22](/articles/s43246-024-00652-8#ref-CR22 "Horwath, J. P., Zakharov, D. N., Mégret, R. & Stach, E. A. Understanding important features of deep learning models for segmentation of high-resolution transmission electron microscopy images. npj Comput. Mater. 6, 108 (2020)."), we tuned an U-net network to automatically discriminate the different intensities found in the experimental images by semantic segmentation. It is worth noting that this segmentation process was conducted under supervision, *i.e*. after training the neural network.

SUMMARY:
More importantly, rational design and controllable synthesis of catalysts have been pushed to a new stage along with the vigorous development in single-atom catalysis.7 Although the development of single-atom catalysts continued to flourish,8,9,10,11,12,13 the catalytic activity still remains to be improved, which may be severely restricted by the density of the isolated metal active centers14,15,16 and their intrinsic activity.17 In fact, arduous efforts have been dedicated to regulate the intrinsic activity, which is based on control and optimization of the energy level structure and surface electronic structure.18 Hence, it can improve the catalytic performance even with a limited number of active sites, which is different from increasing density by adding the number of accessible catalytic active sites.

The bigger picture
------------------

With the technological and scientific progress in synthesis, characterization, and surface science, as well as theoretical and computational chemistry, the development of catalysts has gradually transformed from selection done “blindly” through trial and error to research based on rational design. In recent years, single-atom catalysis and its atom-level regulation research have received enormous interest from academic research, bringing light to atomically precise catalysis. Remarkably, atom-level regulation of single-atom catalysts has a particular focus on the interatomic interfaces and interactions between active atoms, which are expected to offer a comprehensive and deep understanding of catalytic processes through exploration of the potential interatomic synergy effect. By virtue of single-atom catalysts and their atom-level regulation, rational design and controllable synthesis has been pushed to a new stage. In this review, we survey relevant progress in atomic-level regulation strategies, and we summarize the critical factors for effective regulation and improvement of single-atom catalysts.

Summary
-------

Single-atom catalysts have become the forefront and hotspot in catalysis research due to their unique structural characteristics such as extreme atomic utilization efficiency and uniformity of reaction centers. To break through the bottleneck on their intrinsic activity enhancement, arduous efforts have been made to regulate the metal single-atomic sites. Herein, with a special interest in interatomic interfaces and interactions, our review focuses on the atomic-level regulation strategies of single-atom catalysts, including nonmetal [heteroatom doping](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/heteroatom-doping "Learn more about heteroatom doping from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and polymetallic active site construction, referring to the introduction of nonmetal or metal atoms, respectively, into primitive metal single-atomic sites. We summarize the progress of these two strategies in optimizing single-atom active sites from the perspectives of synthesis, application, and mechanism. We start from a systematic overview of the atomic-level regulation methods and corresponding fabrication routes, followed by a comprehensive summary of the current advances in some typical [electrocatalytic reactions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrocatalytic-reaction "Learn more about electrocatalytic reactions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Then we expound on the synergistic reaction mechanism and the intrinsic structure-activity relationship after regulation. In the final part, we discuss the challenges and opportunities of these two atomic-level regulation strategies for further development. Our review of the atomic-level regulation of single-atom catalysts gives significant insight into the rational design of atom-efficient catalysts and adds depth and breadth to the fundamental understanding of atomically precise catalysis by elucidating its intrinsic mechanisms.

Introduction
------------

Single-atom catalysts are a new catalytic material family composed of isolated metal single-atom and support material with distinctive structural properties,[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib1),[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib2) such as isolated metal active centers, extreme dispersion, highly unsaturated coordination environment, and uniform reaction centers.[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib3),[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib4),[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib5),[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib6) By virtue of their unique structural characteristics, single-atom catalysis has been regarded as a pioneer of atomically precise catalysis. More importantly, rational design and controllable synthesis of catalysts have been pushed to a new stage along with the vigorous development in single-atom catalysis.[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib7) Although the development of single-atom catalysts continued to flourish,[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib8),[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib9),[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib10),[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib11),[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib12),[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib13) the catalytic activity still remains to be improved, which may be severely restricted by the density of the isolated metal active centers[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib14),[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib15),[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib16) and their intrinsic activity.[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib17) In fact, arduous efforts have been dedicated to regulate the intrinsic activity, which is based on control and optimization of the energy level structure and surface electronic structure.[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib18) Hence, it can improve the catalytic performance even with a limited number of active sites, which is different from increasing density by adding the number of accessible catalytic active sites.

In recent years, atomic-level regulation strategies for single-atom catalysts have received enormous interest from the academic research community. Inspired by the [heteroatom](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/heteroatom "Learn more about heteroatom from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") doping modification, a common strategy is to introduce nonmetal heteroatoms with different [electronegativity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electronegativity "Learn more about electronegativity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") from the atoms in the original support, such as N, S, P, O, B, etc.,[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib19),[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib20) called nonmetal [heteroatom doping](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/heteroatom-doping "Learn more about heteroatom doping from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") strategy. In fact, the active center of single-atom catalysts usually refers to the collective entity of the central metal single atom and the coordination atoms from the support, in which the coordination atoms exert unprecedented influence on the formation, stability, and overall catalytic performance of single-atom metal active centers,[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib3),[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib21) and become a more promising point for catalysts modification. Nonmetal heteroatom doping strategy introduces nonmetal heteroatoms so as to change the type or number of coordination atoms, which can achieve a significant change on the [oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxidation-reaction "Learn more about oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") state, electronic structure, and local spatial configuration of the central metal atom.[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib17) Furthermore, doped nonmetal heteroatoms may participate in the process of adsorption for reaction molecules and assist the formation of reaction intermediates, so as to adjust and enhance the catalytic performance.

In addition, another atomic-level regulation strategy is called construction of polymetallic active sites, derived from the adjustment mechanism of metal alloy structure.[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib17) It introduces other atomic metal site into the single-atom catalysts for catalytic performance enhancement. To some extent, construction of polymetallic active sites can be regarded as metal atom doping, and is expected to further improve the activity and selectivity by their unique multi-metal active site structure. Up to now, the dual-atom catalysts (DACs) constructed by introducing only another one atomic metal site are a typical representative among them.[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib22) By formation of a homonuclear or heteronuclear dual-atom metal site, it might be possible to modulate the catalytic performance through various interatomic interactions (such as bifunctional effects, electronic structure reconstruction, and geometric configuration regulation).[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib23)

Atomic-level regulation strategies, including nonmetal heteroatom doping and polymetallic active site construction, are of great importance not only for fundamental research but also for practical application. (1) Firstly, atomic-level regulation based on defined active centers from single-atom catalysts constructs the new active centers with more precise atomic numbers and configurations than other methods. Consequently, it becomes a viable way to provides ideal models for structure-property investigation, bringing light to atomically precise catalysis.[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib24) (2) Secondly, it has a particular focus on the interatomic interfaces and interactions between active atoms, which are expected to acquire a comprehensive and deep understanding of the catalytic processes by exploring the [potential interatomic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/interatomic-potential "Learn more about potential interatomic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") synergy effect. (3) Thirdly, it has been demonstrated to effectively enhance the catalytic activity and selectivity in many reactions,[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib23),[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib25),[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib26),[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib27),[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib28),[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib29),[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib30),[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib31) especially in the [electrochemical reactions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrochemical-reaction "Learn more about electrochemical reactions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Herein, this review predominantly aims at elucidating the atomic-level regulation strategies of single-atom catalysts, by which we are referring to atomic modification on the primitive metal single-atomic site by introducing nonmetal or metal atoms, respectively (shown as [Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#fig1)). More specifically, we start from a systematic overview on their fabrication routes and methods, and next summarize their current advances applied in some typical [electrocatalytic reactions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrocatalytic-reaction "Learn more about electrocatalytic reactions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), such as ORR, OER, HER, CO 2 RR, and so on. Then, we expound the synergistic reaction mechanism and the intrinsic structure–activity relationship after regulation. At the end of this review, we present the challenges and opportunities of these two atomic-level regulation strategies. Along with ceaseless innovation and maturation of its technology, atomic-level regulation for single-atom catalysts would significantly facilitate the rational design of high-efficiency nanocatalysts, and sharpens our understanding of atomically precise catalysis by elucidating the interatomic synergy mechanisms.

Nonmetal heteroatom doping
--------------------------

Nonmetal [heteroatom](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/heteroatom "Learn more about heteroatom from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") doping strategy is a typical strategy in the atomic-level regulation for single-atom catalysts, especially for carbon-based single-atom catalysts. [Carbon material](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/carbon-material "Learn more about Carbon material from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a kind of environmental-friendly material with high performance,[70](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib70) for its specific features such as high specific [surface area](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/surface-area "Learn more about surface area from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), superb chemical and mechanical dependability, adjustable morphological and structural combinations, tunable porosity and surface properties, and good electrical and [thermal conductivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/thermal-conductivity "Learn more about thermal conductivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").[71](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib71) Hence, carbon-based material can achieve different functions by ingenious design and precision synthesis for [chemical structure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/structure-composition "Learn more about chemical structure from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which is commonly used as metal-free catalysts or as a support in single-atom catalysts, and widely applied and researched in the field of catalysis, energy, ecology, and so on.[71](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib71),[72](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib72),[73](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib73) However, with the primitive carbon material there is difficulty in providing strong metal-support interaction to anchor isolate metal atoms,[74](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib74) making unsuitable to be directly used as a support material for preparation of single-atom catalysts.[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib17) Defects or heteroatom sites act as anchor sites for isolated metal atoms through strong metal-support interaction. The construction of carbon defect sites to coordinate with metal atoms is a relatively complex process, while it is easy to implement to dope nonmetallic heteroatoms in carbon materials.

Compared with the primitive carbon material, [heteroatom doping](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/heteroatom-doping "Learn more about heteroatom doping from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can effectively regulate the performance of the material and provide more suitable anchoring sites for the isolated metal atom, and play a role in regulating the [oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxidation-reaction "Learn more about oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") state and electronic structure of the central metal atom. The N element has high similarity and compatibility with the C element, so it has been the most deeply studied doped element. [N doping](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/n-type-doping "Learn more about N doping from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") cannot only increase the conductivity of carbon materials, but also improve the activity of carbon-based materials.[75](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib75) Recently, many studies have been carried out on the structure of multiple heteroatoms. These works are mostly carried out on the basis of N atom doping, introducing a second kind of heteroatom, such as S,[76](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib76) P,[77](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib77) O,[78](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib78) B,[79](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib79) etc. By participating in central metal-support coordination, heteroatom doping is able to reduce the high surface energy of the isolated metal atom, which not only stabilizes the highly dispersed metal single-atom active sites but also modulates the activity. It can also be used as an "environment atom" to replace the original carbon atom in the skeleton, and there is a long-range interaction between the support and the metal single-atom active site, effectively improving the catalytic activity of the catalyst. For example, Xie’s group reported a strategy of sulfur doping (S doping) to achieve effective regulation of the [electrocatalytic activity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrocatalytic-activity "Learn more about electrocatalytic activity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the Fe–NC active site.[80](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib80) They have demonstrated atomic dispersion of Fe–N _x_ species on N and S codecorated hierarchical carbon layers and employed the material as a [bifunctional catalyst](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/bifunctional-catalyst "Learn more about bifunctional catalyst from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The improvement of the electrocatalytic performance of Fe–N–C active centers after S atom doping was attributed to the uneven distribution of charge caused by doping and the increase of Fe 3d electron conductivity by sulfur substitution.
